---
title: "Methodology & Caveats"
subtitle: "How to read these numbers responsibly"
---

## Scope

This analysis covers January 1999 through the present. Since Bill Clinton's
first full term started in 1993, only the last two years of his second term
appear in the data (1999 – January 2001). Every subsequent administration
is fully covered except the current one, which is ongoing.

## How months are assigned to presidents

Each calendar month is attributed to whichever president was in office on
the **14th of that month**. Because US inaugurations fall on January 20:

- The outgoing president "keeps" January of the handover year (they held
  office for 19 of 31 days).
- The incoming president's first *full* month is February.

This matches the standard academic convention for per-administration
economic comparisons.

## How per-administration aggregates are computed

| Metric | Method |
|---|---|
| GDP growth (avg) | Calendar-year real GDP growth (%YoY), averaged across the calendar years whose July 1 falls in the admin's window |
| Unemployment Δ | Unemployment rate at last month − rate at first month (percentage points) |
| CPI avg | Mean of monthly CPI year-over-year inflation across the admin's months |
| Fed Funds Δ | Fed Funds rate at last month − rate at first month (percentage points) |
| S&P 500 total return | `(last month close / first month close − 1) × 100` |
| S&P 500 annualized | `(end / start)^(1/years) − 1`, with `years = months / 12` |
| VIX avg | Mean of monthly VIX across admin window |
| Recession years | Count of calendar years flagged as NBER recession in the admin window |
| Debt added ($T) | `GFDEBTN` at last quarter in admin window − `GFDEBTN` at first quarter (converted from $M to $T) |
| Δ Debt/GDP (pp) | `GFDEGDQ188S` at last quarter − at first quarter (percentage points) |
| Avg deficit (% GDP) | Mean of annual `FYFSGDA188S` across calendar years attributed to admin |

Current (ongoing) administrations are flagged with `ongoing = TRUE` in the
CSV and with an asterisk (`*`) in all charts and tables.

## Why these numbers should not be read as presidential "scorecards"

Correlation is not causation, especially here:

1. **Inherited conditions.** Every president takes office with an economy
   shaped by decisions made under predecessors, sometimes decades earlier.
   The 2008 crisis began 6 months before Obama's term; COVID-19 arrived in
   Trump I's fourth year; post-pandemic inflation peaked under Biden but was
   driven by global supply-chain and stimulus factors with long lags.

2. **Monetary policy is independent.** The Federal Reserve is structurally
   independent from the White House. Interest-rate decisions — which drive
   most market behavior — are made by the FOMC, not the president.

3. **Global shocks.** Dot-com bust, GFC, COVID-19, wars, oil-price shocks —
   external events typically dwarf the economic impact of any domestic
   executive action.

4. **Policy lag.** Legislation enacted in year N of a term typically affects
   the economy in year N+2 or later. Attributing a term's results to that
   term's policies is usually wrong.

5. **Congress, not the president, writes fiscal policy.** Major spending,
   tax, and trade changes require congressional action.

6. **Selection bias.** Terms vary from 4 to 8 years and overlap with very
   different business-cycle phases. Comparing a term that started at a
   recession trough to one that started at a peak produces radically
   different headline numbers for reasons unrelated to presidential policy.

7. **Fiscal-year vs. presidential-year mismatch.** The federal fiscal year
   runs October to September, and each year's budget is drafted and enacted
   by Congress and signed by the president *before* the fiscal year begins.
   FY2021, for example, was legislated under Trump but executed largely
   under Biden. This report uses a mid-year rule to attribute fiscal data,
   but that convention is imperfect — a president inherits fiscal decisions
   made by their predecessor and Congress.

8. **Mandatory vs. discretionary.** The majority of federal spending
   (Social Security, Medicare, Medicaid, interest on the debt) is
   *mandatory* and continues regardless of who holds office. Presidents
   and Congresses have limited year-to-year control over these flows.

Use these charts as descriptive context, not causal claims.

## Administration reference

```{r admin-ref}
#| echo: false
source("R/helpers.R")
source("R/presidential_helpers.R")

admins <- load_administrations()
library(reactable); library(htmltools)
party_badge <- function(party) {
  col <- party_color(party)
  tags$span(
    style = sprintf(
      "display:inline-block; padding:2px 8px; border-radius:3px; font-size:0.72rem;
       font-weight:600; color:#fff; background:%s;", col),
    toupper(party)
  )
}

reactable(
  admins |> transmute(
    president, party,
    start_date, end_date = ifelse(is.na(end_date), "ongoing", as.character(end_date)),
    duration_days = ifelse(ongoing, paste0(duration_days, " (to date)"), duration_days)
  ),
  striped = TRUE, compact = TRUE, highlight = TRUE,
  theme = reactableTheme(borderColor = "#dee2e6"),
  columns = list(
    president = colDef(name = "President", minWidth = 200),
    party = colDef(name = "Party", minWidth = 120,
                   cell = function(v) party_badge(v), html = TRUE),
    start_date = colDef(name = "Term start"),
    end_date = colDef(name = "Term end"),
    duration_days = colDef(name = "Duration (days)")
  )
)
```

## Data sources

All underlying data comes from the [US Economy analysis](../economy/index.qmd);
see its [Data & Citations page](../economy/about.qmd) for the full source list
(BEA, BLS, Federal Reserve, NBER via FRED). This report only derives admin
slices — it does not introduce any new data source.

## Code license

MIT — see the [LICENSE](https://github.com/sam-caldwell/samcaldwell.info/blob/main/LICENSE)
file in the repository.

## Disclaimer

Nothing on this site constitutes investment, tax, legal, political, or
financial advice. The market data is a calibrated synthetic overlay and
should not be used as a source of record.
